title: Polynomial Capacity and its Applications: To TSP and Beyond
speaker: Jonathan Leake
abstract: About 20 years ago, Gurvits developed the notion of polynomial capacity to give a simple proof of the famous van der Waerden lower bound on the permanent of a doubly stochastic matrix. Since then, similar techniques have led to various other applications; for example: approximate counting of the lattice points of certain polytopes, an efficient algorithm for the non-commutative version of the polynomial identity testing problem, and most recently, an improved approximation factor for the traveling salesperson problem (TSP) via the max-entropy algorithm.

proof_stages.label: Coefficient-and-capacity setup for the permanent
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces the general strategy of encoding a hard quantity as a coefficient of an efficiently evaluable polynomial, then applies it to the permanent via Gurvits’s capacity bound for the all-ones coefficient.

proof_stages.label: Real stability and normalized capacity
proof_stages.summary: Real stability is presented as the multivariate analogue of real-rootedness with closure properties, capacity is shown to become a convex optimization problem, and doubly stochastic normalization yields the van der Waerden permanent bounds.

proof_stages.label: Matrix scaling to doubly stochastic form
proof_stages.summary: Matrix scaling is introduced as the way to reduce a general nonnegative matrix to the doubly stochastic case, with Sinkhorn scaling giving approximate row and column normalization in polynomially many iterations.

proof_stages.label: Robust capacity bounds under approximate marginals
proof_stages.summary: A Gurvits--Nick robust theorem bounds capacity when the gradient at the all-ones vector is close to the desired vector, allowing approximate matrix scaling to still give controlled permanent approximations.

proof_stages.label: Distributional and entropy dictionary for capacity
proof_stages.summary: Stable generating polynomials of probability distributions on subsets are reinterpreted as strongly Rayleigh distributions, with coefficients as probabilities, gradients as marginals, closure operations as conditioning or marginalization, and capacity as a KL-divergence/entropy dual.

proof_stages.label: Max-entropy spanning trees for metric TSP parity
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Christofides’s 3/2-approximation and the max-entropy spanning-tree variant, the speaker explains that TSP improvement requires lower bounds on parity events for random tree degrees, first treating the one-vertex case via a univariate real-rooted polynomial and Hoeffding’s theorem.

proof_stages.label: Multivertex parity from generalized coefficient bounds
proof_stages.summary: For several vertices the parity information becomes a multivariate coefficient problem, and a recent Gurvits--Oveis Gharan--Nick theorem gives lower bounds for general coefficients of real stable polynomials, yielding constant-size multivertex parity bounds needed for the TSP application while exposing remaining limitations.

examples.name: 0-1 permanent as perfect-matchings count
examples.note: The 0-1 case of the permanent is identified with counting perfect matchings in a bipartite graph, illustrating the computational hardness that motivates approximation.
